title: Why TypeScript exists
instruction:
JavaScript is great — until your codebase grows and a typo causes a production bug at 2am. TypeScript catches those bugs **before** your code runs.

This simple example would silently fail in JavaScript. In TypeScript, the error is caught immediately. Run it to see the output.
successMessage: TypeScript guaranteed `salary` and `rate` are numbers — no accidental string concatenation. The compiler is your first line of defense.

title: Your first type annotation
instruction:
The `: type` syntax is a **type annotation**. It tells TypeScript exactly what kind of value a variable holds. Annotate `username` as `string` and `loginCount` as `number`.

Change the `TODO` annotations to the correct types, then run.
successMessage: Four type annotations, four different types. TypeScript now enforces that `loginCount` can never accidentally become `"forty-two"` — a string that would break any math.

title: Type inference — TypeScript reads your mind
instruction:
You don't need to annotate everything. TypeScript **infers** types from the values you assign. Hover over a variable in a real editor and you'll see the inferred type.

Run this and observe — TypeScript knows all three types without being told.
successMessage: TypeScript inferred `price` as `number` — so `price.toFixed(2)` works perfectly. If you tried `price = "free"` it would refuse to compile. That's the value of inference.

title: Functions with typed parameters
instruction:
Typed function parameters are TypeScript's biggest win. They make functions **self-documenting** and prevent entire classes of bugs.

This function builds a user greeting. The types tell you exactly what it expects — no need to read the implementation.
expectedOutput: Welcome back, Alice! You're logged in as admin (age 34).
Welcome back, Bob! You're logged in as editor (age 28).
successMessage: The function signature `(name: string, role: string, age: number): string` is a contract. TypeScript enforces it — pass the wrong type and the build fails before it ever reaches production.

title: The unknown type — safe error handling
instruction:
One real-world gotcha: errors caught in `catch` blocks are typed as `unknown`. You must check the type before using them — TypeScript forces safe error handling.

This pattern is used throughout production TypeScript codebases.
expectedOutput: Error: "abc" is not a valid age
Error: Age -5 is out of range
successMessage: The `error instanceof Error` check is mandatory in TypeScript — `catch (error)` gives you `unknown`, not `Error`. This forces you to handle unexpected error types, making your code genuinely safer.
